progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—how to incorporate planning-like inductive biases into model-free RL without requiring tabular state spaces or explicit environment models—and the framing of XLVINs as combining value-iteration-inspired implicit planning with neural algorithmic reasoning is compelling. The introduction is generally clear, well motivated, and positions the work against relevant prior VI-based planners, especially by identifying the “algorithmic bottleneck” of scalar reward/value prediction as the key limitation the method aims to overcome. However, the central conceptual claim would benefit from sharper exposition: it is not yet fully clear from the opening how latent-space “execution” preserves the useful guarantees or structure of value iteration, what exactly is meant by “alignment” with VI, and whether the bottleneck is a theoretical limitation, an empirical observation, or primarily an intuitive diagnosis. Some claims are quite strong—e.g., that the method addresses “all” limitations of prior work or “breaks” the bottleneck—and should be carefully qualified unless supported rigorously later. The paper’s quality appears high, with a broad empirical ambition across control, navigation, and Atari, but its most significant risks are whether the proposed latent algorithmic reasoning mechanism is sufficiently interpretable and demonstrably tied to planning rather than simply being a more expressive architecture, and whether comparisons to prior implicit planners and model-free baselines are controlled enough to isolate the contribution. Overall, the work is promising and well situated, but the paper should ensure that its key novelty, assumptions, and evidence for genuine VI-like planning are made precise and convincingly validated.

comments.title: Value iteration is described as differentiable and unconditionally guaranteed
comments.quote: As VI is a differentiable algorithm, guaranteed to find the _optimal_ policy, it can combine with gradient-based optimisation and provides useful theoretical guarantees.
comments.explanation: The intended point is understandable: value iteration can be unrolled in neural architectures, and in the classical finite discounted MDP setting with known model parameters it converges to the optimal value function. However, the wording is compressed enough to be misleading. Standard VI includes a max over actions, which is not differentiable at action ties, though it is differentiable almost everywhere or can be softened/subdifferentiated in practice. Its optimality guarantee also depends on assumptions such as a known finite/tabular discounted MDP and convergence of the iterative procedure; it is not a consequence of differentiability itself.

comments.title: Scalar-bottleneck claims are stated more definitively than the evidence supports
comments.quote: This effect is caused by explicitly running the planning algorithm based on scalar predictions in every state, which can be harmful to data efficiency if such scalars are improperly predicted. / As the ATreeC-style approaches commit to using the scalar produced by their reward models, there is no way to recover from a poorly predicted value. / XLVINs ... successfully break the algorithmic bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The paper's motivating argument is sensible: scalar reward/value predictions used inside Bellman-style backups can directly corrupt planned action preferences, especially early in training. However, several formulations overstate the point. Approximate VI with imperfect scalar inputs is not inherently unrecoverable: Bellman backups have error-propagation bounds, small scalar errors may not change the greedy action, and end-to-end training can update or partially compensate for biased scalar heads. Conversely, XLVIN still depends on learned encoders, latent transitions, frozen-executor compatibility, and downstream policy/value heads, so the bottleneck may be shifted or mitigated rather than categorically eliminated. A more defensible claim would be that XLVIN avoids or mitigates the specific scalar-planning interface studied in the paper.

comments.title: Transition model is defined as an additive displacement but later treated as the successor embedding
comments.quote: Specifically, _T_ ( _z_ ( _s_ ) _, a_ ) is a _translation vector_ to be added to _z_ ( _s_ ) in order to obtain an embedding of the resulting state when taking action _a_ in state _s_ .
comments.explanation: The TransE description says that the transition model returns a translation vector, so the successor latent should be of the form z(s) + T(z(s), a). Algorithm 1 appears to confirm this additive interpretation. However, the prose later says that applying T yields successor embeddings directly, which drops the addition and treats T itself as the successor-state predictor. This is not merely cosmetic: a displacement model and a direct successor model have different parameterizations and different interpretations under the TransE loss. The notation should consistently distinguish the translation vector from the resulting successor embedding.

comments.title: VIN-style prior work is described as requiring fixed and known dynamics, which may overstate the assumption
comments.quote: Several implicit planners, including (G)VIN [40, 29] and GPPN [26], were proposed for discrete environments where _P_ is fixed and known.
comments.explanation: The broader contrast is reasonable: VIN/GVIN-style methods often rely on known discrete topology, local neighborhoods, or grid/graph structure, whereas XLVIN learns a latent transition model. But saying that the full transition function P is fixed and known can overstate the assumption. Some such models assume a known neighborhood structure while learning convolutional kernels or transition/reward effects. A more precise taxonomy would distinguish known topology or action neighborhood structure from fully known transition probabilities.

comments.title: Applying VI over the learned latent tree under-specifies transition probabilities and stochastic expectations
comments.quote: Then, VI can be directly applied over the constructed tree to yield the final policy. / The transition function produces state embeddings that correspond to the expectation of the successor state embedding over all possible outcomes ... an operation that aligns well with VI computations.
comments.explanation: Classical VI requires rewards, values, and transition probabilities through the Bellman expectation \(\sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)V(s')\). The learned TransE neighborhood appears to produce one latent successor per action, which implicitly assumes either deterministic transitions or a collapsed expected successor embedding. But an expected latent successor is not generally equivalent to the Bellman expectation: \(V(\mathbb{E}[z(s')])\) need not equal \(\mathbb{E}[V(z(s'))]\) unless strong linearity or representation assumptions hold. Moreover, a TransE loss yields a conditional mean only under particular losses and modeling assumptions. The paper should clarify whether the backup is deterministic, whether transition probabilities are predicted, or whether stochasticity is deliberately collapsed into an expected latent.

comments.title: Executor interface obscures how action labels enter the VI-like backup
comments.quote: The executor function, _X_ : R _[k] ⇥_ R _[|A|⇥][k] !_ R _[k]_ , processes an embedding _[~] hs_ of a given state _s_ , alongside a neighbourhood set _N_ ( _[~] hs_ ), which contains (expected) embeddings of states that immediately neighbour _s_ —for example, through taking actions.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: each action generates a successor embedding, and the executor aggregates over the local graph. However, a Bellman optimality update is action-indexed, and the final policy must preserve which branch corresponds to which action. The formal definition of X and the prose describe inputs as a current embedding plus a neighborhood of neighbor embeddings, but do not clearly state whether action labels, edge features, or a fixed action ordering are provided. Algorithm 1 appears to store action-labeled edges, so the intended design may be recoverable, but it should be explicit for reproducibility and for the claim of VI alignment.

comments.title: Claim of imposing no MDP-structure assumptions is too broad
comments.quote: The entire procedure is end-to-end differentiable, does not impose any assumptions on the structure of the underlying MDP, and has the capacity to perform computations directly aligned with value iteration, while avoiding the algorithmic bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with VIN/GVIN-style methods is reasonable: XLVIN does not require a known grid or transition graph upfront. But stated literally, the claim overreaches. The architecture assumes a finite discrete action set, expands every action from each node, uses a single learned latent transition per state-action pair, and has acknowledged limitations for nondeterministic MDPs and continuous action spaces. The paper should qualify the claim as avoiding a known tabular/topological transition graph, not as imposing no structural assumptions on the MDP.

comments.title: Frozen pretrained processor is not clearly compatible with XLVIN deployment inputs
comments.quote: We only retain the _processor_ as our executor function _X_ ... / Finally, we supervise a GNN ... to receive _Vt_ ( _s_ )—and all other parameters of the MDP—as inputs, and predict _Vt_ +1( _s_ ).
comments.explanation: The idea of pretraining a neural algorithmic processor on synthetic VI traces and reusing it as an executor is appealing. The missing link is how the frozen processor's expected input representation matches the learned embeddings used at deployment. During pretraining, the processor receives representations derived from scalar values and MDP parameters such as rewards, transitions, and possibly discounting. During XLVIN deployment, it receives learned state embeddings and generated latent neighbors, without explicit scalar rewards, transition probabilities, or current value vectors. If the encoder from pretraining is discarded and X is frozen, the paper needs to explain how z and T learn to place states into the coordinate system expected by the processor, and how Bellman-relevant quantities are encoded in those latents. Without this, the claim that the retained processor still executes a VI-like update is underjustified.

comments.title: Negative sampling for TransE should exclude true successors or clarify false negatives
comments.quote: Negative sample states for TransE, ˜ _s_ , are randomly sampled from the rollouts.
comments.explanation: Using rollout states as negatives is a reasonable practical choice, but the description should specify that positives are excluded from the negative pool. Otherwise the contrastive term can create false negatives by pushing the predicted next-state embedding away from an actual successor, especially in discrete domains, repeated states, terminal states, frame-stacked observations, or stochastic transitions. The issue is not negative sampling itself, but the missing clarification needed to ensure that the TransE objective is not self-contradictory.

comments.title: Statement about PPO gradients flowing into the executor is confusing if the executor is frozen
comments.quote: Note that the PPO gradients also flow into _T_ and _X_ , which could _displace_ them from the properties required by the above, leading to either poorly constructed graphs or lack of VI-aligned computation.
comments.explanation: The warning is conceptually sensible if PPO updates all modules end-to-end. However, the next paragraph says that X is pretrained and frozen. Gradients may still backpropagate through a frozen executor to earlier modules, but they should not update X's parameters. The optimization description should distinguish gradients through X from parameter updates to X, and specify whether T remains trainable under both PPO and the TransE loss while X is held fixed.

comments.title: Noise-perturbation experiment is underspecified, uncalibrated, and somewhat overinterpreted
comments.quote: we study the effect of introducing Gaussian noise in the scalar inputs fed to VI compared to introducing Gaussian noise in the high-dimensional latents fed into the XLVIN executor. / this experiment provides direct evidence of the algorithmic bottleneck
comments.explanation: The perturbation experiment is a useful diagnostic, but its interpretation is limited by several missing details. First, the scalar quantities being corrupted are underspecified: noise added to rewards, terminal values, intermediate value iterates, Q-values, or final action values would have different effects. Second, applying the same Gaussian standard deviation to scalars and high-dimensional latents is not automatically comparable because their dimensionalities, semantics, and arbitrary representation scales differ. Third, artificial Gaussian perturbations may not resemble real learned-model errors in scalar-planning agents, which can be biased, correlated, or policy-dependent. The result can support the bottleneck hypothesis, but by itself it does not establish that latent planning is intrinsically more robust to prediction error or provide direct evidence of the full algorithmic bottleneck.

comments.title: A single ATreeC baseline is treated as representative of a broader class of scalar-planning methods
comments.quote: we use “ATreeC” [13] as one of our baselines, capturing the behavior of a larger class of VI-based implicit planners (including TreeQN and VPN [30]).
comments.explanation: ATreeC, TreeQN, and VPN share the relevant feature that they perform planning or backups over learned scalar rewards and/or values, so ATreeC is a plausible representative baseline for the paper's thesis. Still, these methods differ in rollout construction, training objectives, backup operators, representation learning, policy extraction, and scalar supervision. Results for ATreeC illustrate a scalar-planning failure mode but do not necessarily characterize the empirical behavior of the whole class. The phrasing should be softened to say that ATreeC is a representative scalar-planning baseline sharing the identified bottleneck.

comments.title: Linear decodability of optimal values is stronger evidence of value information than of executing VI
comments.quote: after the GNN computations are performed, the recovered state embeddings ... are consistently almost-perfectly linearly decodable to VI outputs _V[?]_ ( _s_ ) ... Hence, the encoder maps _s_ to a latent space from which the executor can perform VI.
comments.explanation: The probe is useful: if optimal values become much more linearly decodable after executor processing, that supports the claim that the executor adds planning-relevant information. But high linear decodability of \(V^*\) from post-executor embeddings does not uniquely identify the computation as value iteration. The executor could learn heuristics correlated with value, encode environment-specific structure, or produce features shaped by PPO objectives. The result is consistent with VI-aligned computation, but the word "Hence" makes the inference stronger than the evidence alone warrants.

comments.title: Claim that XLVIN explores and exploits better is not directly supported by average reward curves
comments.quote: From the inception of the training, the XLVIN model explores and exploits better, consistently remaining ahead of the baseline PPO model in the low-data regime.
comments.explanation: Higher average clipped reward early in training supports a claim of better low-data performance. It does not directly establish the separate mechanisms of better exploration and better exploitation. Average returns can improve due to representation learning, optimization stability, value estimation, inductive bias, or policy learning, and the passage does not report exploration-specific metrics such as state visitation diversity, novelty, coverage, or entropy. The evidence more directly supports improved early reward than a mechanistic exploration/exploitation claim.

comments.title: Conclusion may overgeneralize random-graph pretraining beyond the variants actually covered
comments.quote: The learnt executors are robust and transferable across environments, despite being trained on purely random graphs.
comments.explanation: The claim is directionally consistent with the executor-transfer motivation, especially for the XLVIN-R variant. However, the broad wording could imply that all learned executors used in the paper were trained only on random graphs. If some variants, such as XLVIN-CP, were pretrained on CartPole-style synthetic graphs rather than purely random graphs, the conclusion should qualify the claim as applying to the random-graph-pretrained executor or say that transfer is observed even when training on purely random graphs.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important and timely problem—how to incorporate planning-like inductive biases into model-free RL without requiring tabular state spaces or explicit environment models—and the framing of XLVINs as combining value-iteration-inspired implicit planning with neural algorithmic reasoning is compelling. The introduction is generally clear, well motivated, and positions the work against relevant prior VI-based planners, especially by identifying the “algorithmic bottleneck” of scalar reward/value prediction as the key limitation the method aims to overcome. However, the central conceptual claim would benefit from sharper exposition: it is not yet fully clear from the opening how latent-space “execution” preserves the useful guarantees or structure of value iteration, what exactly is meant by “alignment” with VI, and whether the bottleneck is a theoretical limitation, an empirical observation, or primarily an intuitive diagnosis. Some claims are quite strong—e.g., that the method addresses “all” limitations of prior work or “breaks” the bottleneck—and should be carefully qualified unless supported rigorously later. The paper’s quality appears high, with a broad empirical ambition across control, navigation, and Atari, but its most significant risks are whether the proposed latent algorithmic reasoning mechanism is sufficiently interpretable and demonstrably tied to planning rather than simply being a more expressive architecture, and whether comparisons to prior implicit planners and model-free baselines are controlled enough to isolate the contribution. Overall, the work is promising and well situated, but the paper should ensure that its key novelty, assumptions, and evidence for genuine VI-like planning are made precise and convincingly validated.

comments.title: Value iteration is described as differentiable and unconditionally guaranteed
comments.quote: As VI is a differentiable algorithm, guaranteed to find the _optimal_ policy, it can combine with gradient-based optimisation and provides useful theoretical guarantees.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: value iteration is an algorithmic computation that can be unrolled inside a neural architecture, and in the classical finite discounted MDP setting it converges to the optimal value function when the transition and reward model are known. The following sentences partly resolve the concern by emphasizing that VI assumes a tabular environment with known transition and reward information, and the later background section frames VI for discounted MDPs. What remains slightly problematic is the compressed wording. Standard VI uses a max over actions, which is not differentiable at action ties, though it is differentiable almost everywhere or can be treated with subgradients/soft approximations in neural implementations. Also, the optimality guarantee depends on additional conditions such as an exact finite/tabular discounted MDP model and convergence of the iterative procedure; it is not a consequence of differentiability. This is mostly a framing issue, but a careful reader could object to the sentence as stated.

comments.title: The scalar-prediction bottleneck claim is stated more strongly than justified here
comments.quote: This effect is caused by explicitly running the planning algorithm based on scalar predictions in every state, which can be harmful to data efficiency if such scalars are improperly predicted.
comments.explanation: I take the authors’ intended argument to be that methods such as VPN/ATreeC commit to learned scalar rewards/values before applying Bellman-style backups, so early errors in those scalars can directly corrupt the planned action preferences. The surrounding context makes clear that this is a motivating hypothesis and that later sections study the effect empirically, so it is not merely asserted in isolation. Still, the causal wording is quite strong. Approximate value iteration with imperfect scalar rewards/values is not necessarily doomed in the way implied: Bellman backups have known error-propagation bounds, small scalar errors may not change the greedy action, and end-to-end training can sometimes compensate for biased intermediate predictions. Conversely, XLVIN still depends on learned latent transitions, learned latent representations, and downstream policy/value heads, so replacing scalar backups with high-dimensional latent message passing does not by itself prove that the bottleneck is “broken” rather than shifted. The claim would be more defensible with a qualifier that this is an empirically observed bottleneck in the studied regimes rather than an inherent failure mode of all scalar-planning approaches.

comments.title: Transition model is defined as an additive displacement but later treated as the successor embedding
comments.quote: Specifically, _T_ ( _z_ ( _s_ ) _, a_ ) is a _translation vector_ to be added to _z_ ( _s_ ) in order to obtain an embedding of the resulting state when taking action _a_ in state _s_ .
comments.explanation: The TransE description correctly says that the transition model returns a translation vector, so the successor latent should be something like z(s) + T(z(s), a). I then checked the later prose and the algorithmic context: Algorithm 1 indeed uses _~h[0]_ = _~h_ + _T_ ( _~h, a_ ), which confirms that the intended operation is additive. However, the same passage later says that “applying _T_ ( _[~] hs, a_ ) yields embeddings _[~] hs,a_,” which drops the addition and treats T itself as the successor-state embedding. This is not just a cosmetic distinction: implementing T as a successor predictor versus as a TransE displacement changes the model class and the loss interpretation. The surrounding context resolves the authors’ likely intent, but the notation/prose in this paragraph remains inconsistent and could mislead a reader trying to reconstruct the method.

comments.title: VIN-style prior work is described as requiring fixed and known dynamics, which may overstate the assumption
comments.quote: Several implicit planners, including (G)VIN [40, 29] and GPPN [26], were proposed for discrete environments where _P_ is fixed and known.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ broader point: these methods exploit a known discrete topology or local transition neighbourhood, which is much more restrictive than learning a latent transition model from arbitrary observations. Later in the same paragraph they sharpen this to “the requirement of knowing upfront the transition neighbourhoods,” which is a more defensible statement. The stronger claim that _P_ itself is “fixed and known,” however, is not quite aligned with how some VIN/GVIN-style models are usually presented: they often assume a grid or graph structure and local action connectivity, but may learn convolutional kernels or otherwise approximate transition/reward effects rather than being handed the full transition probability function. Since the paper’s taxonomy contrasts these methods with latent-transition approaches, this overstatement could make the limitation of prior work sound stronger than it is. A more precise formulation would distinguish known state topology/neighbourhood structure from fully known transition probabilities.

comments.title: Applying VI on the learned tree omits how transition probabilities are represented
comments.quote: If we also train a reward model, _R_ ( _[~] hs, a_ ), and a state-value function, _V_ ( _[~] hs_ ) from state embeddings, we can attach scalar values and rewards to our synthesised graph. Then, VI can be directly applied over the constructed tree to yield the final policy.
comments.explanation: The intended comparison to VPN/TreeQN/ATreeC is clear: once scalar rewards and values are attached to a rollout tree, one can perform Bellman-style backups. What gave me pause is that standard VI for an MDP requires not only rewards and successor values but also transition probabilities, as the Bellman update introduced just above contains the expectation term \(\sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a)V(s')\). The constructed TransE neighbourhood described here gives one latent successor per action, so the implicit assumption seems to be a deterministic latent tree, or perhaps an expected successor embedding. Later sections acknowledge limitations of expected latent transitions in nondeterministic MDPs, which partially resolves the concern. Still, in this passage the statement that VI can be “directly applied” after attaching only scalar rewards and values skips an important modelling choice: whether the backup is deterministic, whether probabilities are predicted, or whether stochasticity is collapsed into an expected latent. Without that clarification, a careful reader could incorrectly infer that the learned graph contains all MDP parameters needed for exact VI.

comments.title: Expected latent successor is not generally the Bellman expectation
comments.quote: The transition function produces state embeddings that correspond to the expectation of the successor state embedding over all possible outcomes (Equation 5). While taking expectations is an operation that aligns well with VI computations, it can pose limitations in the case of non-deterministic MDPs.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: a deterministic latent transition model trained on samples from a stochastic MDP will tend to represent some central tendency of possible next-state embeddings, and Bellman backups also involve an expectation over next states. The authors also correctly acknowledge that stochastic MDPs create difficulties for further latent rollouts. What remains problematic is the stronger suggestion that this expected latent embedding is aligned with VI. In VI, the relevant quantity is \(\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)V(s') = \mathbb{E}[V(s')]\), not \(V\big(\mathbb{E}[z(s')]\big)\) or an executor applied to \(\mathbb{E}[z(s')]\). Unless the downstream value/executor computation is linear in the latent representation, or the representation is specially constructed, these are not equivalent by standard Jensen/nonlinearity considerations. Moreover, the TransE-style loss only yields a conditional mean under particular choices such as squared Euclidean loss and no contrastive complications; with other distances or the hinge negative term, the optimum need not literally be the expectation. Thus the stochastic-case caveat is directionally right, but the claimed alignment with VI via expected embeddings is under-justified.

comments.title: Executor interface obscures how action labels enter the VI-like backup
comments.quote: The executor function, _X_ : R _[k] ⇥_ R _[|A|⇥][k] !_ R _[k]_ , processes an embedding _[~] hs_ of a given state _s_ , alongside a neighbourhood set _N_ ( _[~] hs_ ), which contains (expected) embeddings of states that immediately neighbour _s_ —for example, through taking actions.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: each action generates a successor embedding, and the GNN executor aggregates over the resulting local graph. I also noticed that Algorithm 1 stores edges as triples including the action, \((\tilde h,\tilde h',a)\), so the authors may intend action labels to be available internally. However, the formal definition of \(X\) and the prose describe its input as only the current embedding and a neighbourhood set/matrix of neighbour embeddings. A Bellman optimality update is action-indexed: it compares quantities associated with particular actions, and the final policy must assign probabilities to those same actions. If the executor treats neighbours as an unordered set without edge/action features or a fixed action ordering, it is unclear how the computation preserves which branch corresponds to which action. If instead \(\mathbb{R}^{|A|\times k}\) is meant to be ordered by action, or if edge labels are passed as GNN edge features, that should be stated explicitly because it is a substantive part of reproducing a VI-aligned executor rather than a formatting detail.

comments.title: Claim of imposing no MDP-structure assumptions is too broad
comments.quote: The entire procedure is end-to-end differentiable, does not impose any assumptions on the structure of the underlying MDP, and has the capacity to perform computations directly aligned with value iteration, while avoiding the algorithmic bottleneck.
comments.explanation: I read this as contrasting XLVIN with VIN/GVIN-style methods that require a known grid or graph topology, and in that narrower sense the claim is reasonable: the transition model constructs a local latent graph rather than requiring the environment graph upfront. Still, stated literally, it overreaches. The architecture as described assumes a finite discrete action set, expands every action from every node, and uses a single latent translation per state-action pair; the following paragraph itself notes limitations for non-deterministic MDPs, and later discussion says continuous actions would require rollout policies or discretisation. So the method does impose structural assumptions relevant to the MDP/action space, even if it does not assume a known tabular transition graph. A more precise formulation would avoid implying applicability without assumptions to arbitrary MDP structure.

comments.title: Actor output type should specify normalization over actions
comments.quote: The actor function, _A_ : R _[k] ⇥_ R _[k] !_ [0 _,_ 1] _[|A|]_ consumes the state embedding _[~] hs_ and the updated state embedding _~χs_ , producing action probabilities _⇡✓_ ( _a|s_ ) = _A ~hs, ~χs_[Lastly, note that we may] ⇣ ⌘ _a_[, specifying the policy to be followed by our XLVIN agent.]
comments.explanation: The surrounding prose makes the intended object clear: \(A\) is an actor head producing a categorical policy over actions, presumably via a softmax. However, the displayed type \([0,1]^{|A|}\) only says each component lies between 0 and 1; it does not require the components to sum to one. Earlier the paper defines \(\pi(a\mid s)\) as a conditional probability distribution, so the mathematically correct codomain would be the probability simplex \(\Delta^{|A|-1}\), or the text should state that a softmax/normalization is applied. This is easy to fix and probably not a methodological problem, but as written the formal signature is slightly inconsistent with the policy definition.

comments.title: “No way to recover” from scalar prediction errors overstates the bottleneck
comments.quote: As the ATreeC-style approaches commit to using the scalar produced by their reward models, there is no way to recover from a poorly predicted value.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended bottleneck argument is sensible: if a planner explicitly runs VI-style computation over learned scalar rewards/values, inaccurate scalar estimates can directly corrupt the computed plan, especially early in low-data training. That is a legitimate concern. The phrase “there is no way to recover,” however, is stronger than what follows from the setup. In end-to-end actor-critic systems, scalar reward/value heads can be updated by later data and gradients, and downstream policy/value components may partially compensate for systematic errors depending on the architecture. The more defensible claim is that the planning computation at a given forward pass is constrained by those scalar predictions, not that the overall learning system has literally no recovery mechanism. This is a framing overclaim rather than a fatal flaw.

comments.title: Frozen pretrained processor is not clearly connected to XLVIN’s learned latent embeddings
comments.quote: Such a graph neural network has three parts: an _encoder_ , mapping _Vt_ ( _s_ ) to a latent representation, a _processor_ , which performs a step of VI in the latent space, and a _decoder_ , which decodes back _Vt_ +1( _s_ ) from the latent space. We only **retain** the _processor_ as our executor function _X_ , in order to avoid the algorithmic bottleneck in our architecture.
comments.explanation: The intent is clear and appealing: pre-train a neural algorithmic reasoner on synthetic VI traces, then reuse only the latent processor so that XLVIN avoids decoding to scalar values during planning. However, after checking the surrounding architecture, the deployed executor _X_ receives XLVIN state embeddings _\tilde h_s=z(s)_ and generated neighbour embeddings from _T_, not the latent states produced by the pre-training encoder that mapped scalar VI quantities and MDP parameters into the processor’s hidden space. In neural algorithmic reasoning, the processor’s learned dynamics are usually meaningful relative to the representation produced by its encoder. If that encoder is discarded and _X_ is frozen, it is not explained how the online encoder _z_ and transition model _T_ learn to place embeddings in the same latent coordinate system expected by the frozen processor, especially since their online supervision is PPO plus TransE rather than direct VI-state supervision. This does not make the method impossible—the policy/value losses could in principle adapt _z_ and _T_ to the frozen processor—but the paper’s stated mechanism for preserving VI alignment is underjustified here. A careful reader is left unsure why the retained processor should still execute a VI-like update on arbitrary learned environment embeddings.

comments.title: Inputs used to pre-train the VI processor differ from the inputs available at deployment
comments.quote: Finally, we **supervise** a GNN (operating over the MDP transitions as edges) to receive _Vt_ ( _s_ )—and all other parameters of the MDP—as inputs, and predict _Vt_ +1( _s_ ) (optimised using mean-squared error).
comments.explanation: For the synthetic VI pre-training task, it is natural that the GNN must receive enough information to compute the Bellman update, including rewards, transition structure/probabilities, discounting, and current values. The concern is that the deployed XLVIN executor described earlier does not receive explicit scalar rewards, transition probabilities, or current scalar values; it receives only high-dimensional node embeddings and their generated latent neighbourhood. The authors’ likely intent is that these quantities are implicitly encoded in the latent vectors learned by _z_ and _T_. But the passage does not specify how the features used during pre-training are represented inside the processor, which parts are node features versus edge features, or how deployment-time latent embeddings are made compatible with those pre-training inputs. Since the Bellman update depends crucially on rewards and transition probabilities, this mismatch is more than a cosmetic detail: it affects the interpretation of the frozen processor as a VI executor and makes the method difficult to reproduce from this description alone.

comments.title: Negative sampling for TransE should exclude true successors or otherwise clarify false negatives
comments.quote: Negative sample states for TransE, ˜ _s_ , are randomly sampled from the rollouts.
comments.explanation: Using rollout states as negatives is a reasonable practical choice, and the earlier TransE loss makes clear that negatives are meant to be states whose embeddings should be far from the predicted successor. However, the passage says only that negatives are randomly sampled from the rollouts. Unless the implementation excludes the observed successor _s_{t+1}_ and other valid successors of the same state-action pair, the contrastive term can create false negatives: it may push the predicted next-state embedding away from an actual successor. In deterministic continuous-control environments the probability of exact accidental collision may be small, but in discrete or pixel environments with repeated states, terminal states, frame stacking, or stochastic transitions, this can occur more plausibly. Standard contrastive/TransE setups usually specify that positives are excluded from the negative pool. The issue is therefore not the use of negative sampling itself, but the missing clarification needed to ensure the TransE objective is well-defined and not self-contradictory.

comments.title: Statement about PPO gradients flowing into _X_ is confusing once _X_ is frozen
comments.quote: Note that the PPO gradients also flow into _T_ and _X_ , which could _displace_ them from the properties required by the above, leading to either poorly constructed graphs or lack of VI-aligned computation.
comments.explanation: The warning is conceptually sensible: if PPO updates all modules end-to-end, it could move the transition model away from TransE-like dynamics or move the executor away from its VI-like pre-training. The next paragraph partially resolves this by saying that _X_ is pre-trained and frozen. But read literally, the quoted sentence says PPO gradients flow into _X_, while the actual training procedure appears to treat _X_ as constant. Gradients may still backpropagate through the frozen executor to earlier modules, but they should not update _X_’s parameters. This is a small but real ambiguity in the optimization description, especially because _\theta_ was introduced earlier as the policy-network parameters and could be read as including all XLVIN modules. A clearer statement would distinguish gradients through _X_ from parameter updates to _X_, and specify whether _T_ remains trainable under both PPO and TransE while _X_ is frozen.

comments.title: Sensitivity comparison uses an uncalibrated noise scale across scalars and latent vectors
comments.quote: Hence, we study the effect of introducing Gaussian noise in the scalar inputs fed to VI compared to introducing Gaussian noise in the high-dimensional latents fed into the XLVIN executor. In Figure 5, we plot the policy accuracy as a function of noise standard deviation, showing that, while XLVIN is unable to predict policies perfectly at zero noise, it quickly dominates the VI’s policy predictions once the noise magnitude increases.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended diagnostic: if scalar reward/value estimates are a bottleneck, then perturbing those scalars should damage VI-style planning more quickly than perturbing a richer latent representation damages XLVIN’s executor. That is a reasonable qualitative experiment. However, the stated comparison is hard to interpret as evidence for the stronger claim because the same Gaussian noise standard deviation is apparently applied to quantities with different semantics, dimensionalities, and arbitrary scales. A scalar value has a meaningful scale determined by rewards and discounting, whereas latent coordinates can be rescaled by the encoder/executor without changing the downstream policy unless the architecture or normalization fixes that scale. In high dimensions, per-coordinate noise also corresponds to a total perturbation whose norm grows with dimension, so it is unclear whether the latent perturbation is larger, smaller, or comparable to the scalar perturbation. To make the conclusion robust, the experiment would need calibration, e.g. perturbations normalized by each representation’s empirical variance, by action-gap scale, by actual model prediction errors, or by policy-relevant changes. As written, Figure 5 can show robustness under one arbitrary parameterization, but it does not by itself establish that high-dimensional latent planning is intrinsically less sensitive to prediction error than scalar VI inputs.

comments.title: The perturbed “scalar inputs fed to VI” are underspecified
comments.quote: we study the effect of introducing Gaussian noise in the scalar inputs fed to VI compared to introducing Gaussian noise in the high-dimensional latents fed into the XLVIN executor.
comments.explanation: The surrounding context makes the purpose of this experiment clear: isolate the algorithmic bottleneck by comparing scalar-value corruption with latent corruption on synthetic MDPs where the optimal policy is known. What remains unclear is exactly which scalar quantities are being corrupted. Classical VI uses rewards, transition probabilities, and a current value vector; learned scalar planners such as ATreeC/TreeQN/VPN may use predicted rewards, predicted terminal values, Q-values, or TD(\lambda)-style backups. Adding noise to rewards, to initialized/terminal values, to every intermediate value iterate, or directly to final action-values would produce different sensitivities, especially because policy accuracy depends strongly on action gaps. Without specifying the scalar input, whether the transition model is exact, whether noise is added once or at every iteration/node, and how TD(\lambda) is instantiated, the experiment is difficult to reproduce and its connection to the claimed bottleneck in ATreeC-style agents is ambiguous.

comments.title: A single ATreeC baseline is treated as representative of a broader class of scalar-planning methods
comments.quote: we use **“ATreeC”** [13] as one of our baselines, capturing the behavior of a larger class of VI-based implicit planners (including TreeQN and VPN [30]).
comments.explanation: The authors are right that ATreeC, TreeQN, and VPN share an important structural feature relevant to the paper’s thesis: they perform planning/backups over learned scalar rewards and/or values, so they are all plausible examples of the proposed algorithmic bottleneck. Still, the wording risks overstating what a comparison to ATreeC can establish. These methods differ in training objective, rollout construction, backup operator, policy extraction, representation learning, and value/reward supervision. Therefore, ATreeC can illustrate the scalar-bottleneck failure mode, but it does not necessarily capture the empirical behavior of TreeQN or VPN as a class. A more careful phrasing would say that ATreeC is used as a representative scalar-planning baseline sharing the bottleneck, rather than implying that its results characterize all such methods.

comments.title: Linear decodability of \(V^*\) is stronger evidence of value information than of executing VI
comments.quote: after the GNN computations are performed, the recovered state embeddings ( _~χs_ ; in red) are consistently almost-perfectly linearly decodable to VI outputs _V[?]_ ( _s_ ) ( _R_[2] _⇡_ 1). Hence, the encoder maps _s_ to a latent space from which the executor can perform VI.
comments.explanation: The experiment is a useful qualitative probe: if \(V^*(s)\) becomes much more linearly decodable after the executor, that supports the claim that the executor is adding planning-relevant information. However, the inference in the final sentence is somewhat stronger than what the measurement alone proves. High linear decodability of optimal values from \(\tilde\chi_s\) shows that the post-executor embedding contains value information, but it does not uniquely identify the computation as value iteration. The GNN might be learning a heuristic correlated with shortest-path distance, encoding level-specific structure, or producing features shaped by PPO/actor losses rather than literally emulating Bellman updates. The surrounding text presents this as a qualitative study, which helps, but the word “Hence” makes the conclusion sound more definitive than the evidence warrants. A softer conclusion—e.g. that the result is consistent with VI-aligned computation—would be better supported.

comments.title: Claim that XLVIN “explores and exploits better” is not directly supported by average reward curves
comments.quote: From the inception of the training, the XLVIN model explores and exploits better, consistently remaining ahead of the baseline PPO model in the low-data regime (matching it in the latter stages of Enduro).
comments.explanation: The reported result—higher average clipped reward early in Atari training—is relevant and supports a claim of better low-data performance. What it does not directly establish is the decomposition into better exploration and better exploitation. Average return curves can improve because of exploration, representation learning, optimization stability, value estimation, or policy bias, and the passage does not describe exploration-specific metrics such as state visitation diversity, novelty, coverage, or action entropy. The authors may intend “explores and exploits better” informally, but a careful reader could interpret it as a mechanistic claim. The evidence described here supports ‘achieves higher clipped reward in the low-data regime’ more directly than it supports improved exploration and exploitation separately.

comments.title: Noise-perturbation result is framed as direct evidence of the full algorithmic bottleneck
comments.quote: this experiment provides direct evidence of the algorithmic bottleneck: errors in scalar inputs to an algorithm can impact its predictions substantially, while a high-dimensional latent space executor is able to more gracefully handle such perturbations.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended connection: the earlier discussion defines the algorithmic bottleneck as scalar reward/value prediction errors propagating through VI-style computation, and the described perturbation experiment seems designed to isolate exactly that sensitivity. However, if the experiment consists of injecting Gaussian noise into scalar VI inputs versus high-dimensional XLVIN latents, it directly tests robustness to a particular artificial perturbation distribution, not necessarily the actual learned-model errors encountered by ATreeC/VPN-style scalar planners during RL training. Learned scalar errors may be biased, correlated across states/actions, policy-dependent, or structurally different from Gaussian noise. Conversely, high-dimensional latent perturbations may not correspond to errors induced by the learned transition/encoder. Thus the experiment is good supporting evidence for the proposed mechanism, but calling it “direct evidence of the algorithmic bottleneck” slightly overstates what the perturbation study establishes unless the paper also shows that real scalar-prediction errors in the compared planners have the same effect.

comments.title: Conclusion appears to generalize random-graph pretraining beyond the variants actually covered
comments.quote: The learnt executors are robust and transferable across environments, despite being trained on purely random graphs.
comments.explanation: The claim is directionally consistent with the paper’s executor-transfer motivation: pretraining a GNN processor on synthetic VI tasks and freezing it is meant to yield a reusable executor. I also understand that XLVIN-R specifically uses random deterministic graphs, so the sentence may be summarizing that variant. What remains potentially misleading is the broad wording “The learnt executors” together with “trained on purely random graphs.” From the surrounding context, at least one variant, XLVIN-CP, is pre-trained on CartPole-style synthetic graphs rather than purely random graphs, and the full experimental story seems to include multiple executor pretraining regimes. Unless all results supporting robustness/transferability are from the random-graph-pretrained executor alone, the conclusion should qualify this as “some executors,” “the XLVIN-R executor,” or “even when trained on purely random graphs.” As written, a careful reader could infer that every executor used in the paper was trained only on purely random graphs.

comments.title: “Break the algorithmic bottleneck” is stronger than the empirical evidence can strictly establish
comments.quote: XLVINs represent, to the best of our knowledge, one of the first times neural algorithmic executors are used for implicit planning, and they successfully break the algorithmic bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The architectural point is clear and well motivated: XLVIN avoids explicitly running VI over learned scalar reward/value predictions and instead performs VI-aligned computation in latent space, so it removes the particular scalar interface that the authors identify as a bottleneck in prior methods. However, “successfully break the algorithmic bottleneck” reads as a fairly definitive claim. The experiments appear to show improved robustness/data efficiency and qualitative VI alignment, but they do not rule out analogous bottlenecks in the learned encoder, TransE transition model, frozen executor compatibility, or actor/value tail. In other words, XLVIN bypasses the scalar-planning bottleneck by design and empirically mitigates the studied failure mode; it does not necessarily eliminate all algorithmic bottlenecks in planning-like computation. A more precise conclusion would be that XLVINs “avoid” or “mitigate the scalar algorithmic bottleneck identified in prior VI-based implicit planners.”
